You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 2 has a black shape with two chambers. Squares are in the upper chamber at rows 14-17, cols 22-25 and 38-41. They can move UP to row 6, DOWN to row 14, LEFT/RIGHT to move apart/together. Red dots at (38,22), (38,38), (41,17) might be targets. Squares seem trapped in upper chamber. Need to try SPACE or MOUSE.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.

Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE had no impact. Let me think about this differently.
Looking at the image, I see the black shape has openings in the middle section (rows 18-25). The squares are in the upper chamber. But wait - looking at the ASCII more carefully:
- Row 18: MMMMMMBBBBBBBBMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMppppppppppppppppppBBBBBBBBpppppp
- The left side has black at cols 6-11, then magenta at cols 12-31
- The right side has black at cols 48-57, then purple at cols 58-63
So the black shape has "arms" extending left (cols 6-11) and right (cols 48-57) in the middle section. The middle area (cols 12-47) is background.
But the squares are in the upper chamber at cols 22-25 and 38-41. They can't reach the middle section because there's a black wall at rows 14-17.
Wait, but looking at the image, the black shape seems to have a continuous vertical passage in the middle. Let me re-examine the ASCII:
- Row 14: MMMMMMBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBSSSSBBBBMMppBBBBSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBpppppp
- Row 18: MMMMMMBBBBBBBBMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMppppppppppppppppppBBBBBBBBpppppp
At row 14, the squares are at cols 22-25 (left) and 38-41 (right). The black wall is at cols 6-21 and 26-31 on the left, and cols 32-37 and 42-57 on the right.
At row 18, the black wall is at cols 6-11 (left) and 48-57 (right). The middle area cols 12-47 is background.
So the black shape has a gap in the middle section. The squares are contained in the upper chamber, but the chamber has an opening at the bottom (rows 18-25).
But the squares can only move within the upper chamber because of the black walls. They can't move down past row 14.
Hmm, maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the squares or on some other part of the board to trigger something?
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the problem. In Level 1, the goal was to move the squares to the same row and then press RIGHT to make them meet. In Level 2, maybe the goal is different.
Looking at the image, I see:
1. Two sky blue squares in the "eyes"
2. A black shape with a face-like structure
3. Red/black striped patterns at the bottom
4. Magenta/purple horizontal bars in the middle
Maybe the goal is to make the "face" look complete by moving the squares to specific positions? Or maybe the goal is to change the red/black patterns?
Let me try MOUSE to click on different parts of the board to see what happens. I'll start by clicking on one of the squares.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: SPACE had no impact. Squares are trapped in upper chamber. Need to try MOUSE to interact with the board. Let me explore MOUSE interactions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try MOUSE on the left square (around row 15, col 23)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 15, 'col': 23}])
print(f"After MOUSE on left square: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"Squares: {sorted(positions)}")

# Check for new objects
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'R' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        bid = n['boundary']
        print(f"  Red dot at ({bid[0][0]},{bid[0][1]})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
